_note: Ernest article_drill batch 1 — 15 items at B1, distribution weighted toward phantom_article (5) per Ernest's measured weak pattern from coach_notes ('articles — uncountable nouns at 0%'). Themes: school, basketball, friends, sports, books, gym. EN coach language (Ernest's profile setting since 2026-05-01). Single-blank scenes; chip UX (a/an/the/—). Reasoning shown after every answer.

exercises.sentence_template: I bought a new basketball yesterday. {1} ball is much better than my old one.

blanks.reasoning: Second mention. The ball was introduced with 'a' (any new basketball) — once it's known to both of us, switch to 'the'. Russian 'мяч' gives no marker, so this swap is the highest-frequency error.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Second-mention rule: introduce with 'a', refer back with 'the'.

exercises.sentence_template: We arrived at the school gym and waited for {1} coach to arrive.

blanks.reasoning: Situational unique. Once 'the school gym' is established, 'the coach' means the one for that team — both of us know which. Heuristic: 'Do we both know which one?' → yes → THE.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Once a place is established, people and things at that place take 'the'.

exercises.sentence_template: I'm reading {1} first chapter of a really good book.

blanks.reasoning: Inherited specificity. Even though we just introduced the book with 'a', 'first chapter' is inherently unique within it. Pattern: ordinals (first, second, last) + N + of usually take 'the'.

exercises.sentence_template: Did you tell {1} guys about Saturday's training?

blanks.reasoning: Shared knowledge — 'the guys' = our friend group, the basketball team — both of us know which. Heuristic: 'Do we both know which ones?' → yes → THE.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Specific people we both know about take 'the'.

exercises.sentence_template: I made {1} good progress in maths this term.

blanks.reasoning: 'Progress' is uncountable in English — no 'a/an', even with an adjective. Russian 'прогресс' is countable, so 'a good progress' is a direct calque. ✓ 'made good progress' / 'made significant progress' / 'made great progress'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Uncountable nouns (progress, advice, information) take no 'a/an' — even with an adjective.

exercises.sentence_template: Can you give me {1} information about the school trip?

blanks.reasoning: 'Information' is uncountable — no 'a/an', no plural. Russian 'информация' is countable in some uses, so 'an information' is a calque trap. ✓ 'some information' / 'a piece of information'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Information / advice / news / homework / progress — all uncountable, no 'a/an'.

exercises.sentence_template: I have {1} homework to do tonight.

blanks.reasoning: 'Homework' is uncountable — no 'a/an', no plural ('homeworks' doesn't exist). Russian 'домашнее задание' is countable, so 'a homework' is a direct calque. ✓ 'some homework' / 'a lot of homework' / 'two hours of homework'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Homework is uncountable. ✗ a homework / homeworks. ✓ some homework / a lot of homework.

exercises.sentence_template: We're having a basketball match {1} next Friday.

blanks.reasoning: next/last + day/time = no article. ✗ 'the next Friday' / 'a next Friday'. ✓ 'next Friday' / 'last Saturday' / 'next week'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Time expressions with next/last take zero article.

exercises.sentence_template: {1} school can be tough sometimes, but I like it.

blanks.reasoning: Abstract noun in general sense — 'school' here means school-as-an-experience, no article. Russian 'школа' sits naturally as a subject, which makes the urge to add 'the' feel right but it's wrong for the general meaning.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Abstract / institutional nouns in general sense (life, school, music) take no article.

exercises.sentence_template: Our school year ends in {1} June.

blanks.reasoning: Months take no article after 'in'. ✗ 'in the June' / 'in a June'. ✓ 'in June' / 'in summer' / 'in 2026'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: in + month / season / year = no article on the time word.

exercises.sentence_template: The first day of {1} school is always a bit nerve-wracking.

blanks.reasoning: 'School' here is the institutional sense (the experience of school), no article. The phrase 'first day of school' is a fixed idiom. ✓ 'first day of school' / 'last day of school' / 'after school'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Idiomatic 'X of school' (first day of, end of) keeps zero on 'school'.

exercises.sentence_template: We need to take {1} action to clean up the gym after training.

blanks.reasoning: 'Take action' is a fixed phrase — no article. ✗ 'take the action' / 'take an action'. Other zero-article fixed phrases: 'make progress', 'give advice', 'reach agreement'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Fixed phrases (take action, make progress, give advice) carry zero article.

exercises.sentence_template: I have a maths exam at {1} school tomorrow morning.

blanks.reasoning: 'At school' (as a student / the institution sense) takes zero article. Same with 'at work', 'at home', 'at university'. ⚠ 'at the school' would mean physically at the school building (e.g. as a visitor).

exercises.fallback_feedback: Institution role sense (at school, at work, in hospital) takes zero article.

exercises.sentence_template: My older brother is {1} engineer at a tech company.

blanks.reasoning: Professions take 'a/an' — classifying A (saying what kind of person). ✗ 'is engineer' (calque from Russian 'Он инженер'). ✓ 'is an engineer' / 'is a teacher' / 'is a doctor'. ('engineer' starts with a vowel sound → 'an'.)

exercises.fallback_feedback: Person + 'is' + a/an + profession. Russian drops the article — English requires it.

exercises.sentence_template: My best friend got {1} new bike for his birthday.

blanks.reasoning: First mention of a singular countable noun — needs 'a'. ✗ 'got new bike' (Russian 'получил новый велосипед' has no article). ✓ 'got a new bike'. Heuristic: 'Do we both know which one?' → no, it's new info → A.

exercises.fallback_feedback: First mention of a singular countable noun needs 'a' (or 'an' before vowel sounds).
